title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Water
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Canada's CSE reveals a pro-Russia group claimed control-level access to a Quebec water plant — pumps, chlorine dosing and pressure allegedly within reach
items.content_text: In its 2025–2026 annual report, Canada's Communications Security Establishment disclosed that pro-Russia hacktivist group NoName057(16) claimed it covertly controlled an unnamed Quebec municipality's water treatment plant — pumps, chlorine dosing, pressure settings and the monitoring and alerting operators rely on. CSE learned of the activity only from the group's own public boast; the utility never detected it, which is the most telling detail for every other water system. The claimed level of control is unverified and such groups routinely exaggerate, but a national cyber authority featuring the claim is itself the signal. Treat it as a template threat rather than a one-off, and assume small, under-monitored plants are exactly where this plays out.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CSE / Canadian Centre for Cyber Security
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed 30 Jun 2026 — NoName claim, not confirmed

items.title: CISA/FBI/EPA/DOE: Iranian-affiliated actors keep hitting internet-exposed Allen-Bradley PLCs — with confirmed operational disruption
items.content_text: U.S. agencies continue to warn that Iranian-affiliated actors are exploiting internet-facing Rockwell Automation/Allen-Bradley programmable logic controllers across water and wastewater, energy and government-facilities sectors, a campaign re-emphasized amid heightened Middle East tensions. Unlike inflated hacktivist claims, this activity is agency-confirmed: reporting describes malicious interaction with project files, manipulation of data shown on HMI/SCADA displays, and in some cases real operational disruption. The actors are reaching PLCs left exposed on the public internet, often protected only by default or weak credentials, rather than burning novel exploits — which makes the fix largely one of hygiene and exposure reduction.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CISA / FBI / EPA / DOE joint alert
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Ongoing — re-warned late Jun 2026 amid Mideast tensions

items.title: Cal Water finds no OT breach after Iran-linked Handala claimed it could cut the water supply — a textbook “claimed vs confirmed” case
items.content_text: California Water Service investigated a breach claimed by the Iran-linked group Handala and reported finding no evidence of activity in its operational-technology environment. Handala — which presents as hacktivist but is widely assessed to front Iranian state operations — asserted it could have disrupted the water supply and leaked roughly 5 GB of data. Reviewers found the group had actually reached a customer billing database and an internal GPS caster used by field crews; neither treats or distributes water, and independent assessments say nothing supports the shutdown claim. It is a clean reminder that hacktivist “we could turn off your water” claims frequently outrun the actual access — but an IT-side foothold still warrants urgent containment and a hard look at IT-to-OT pathways.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Reported late Jun 2026 — OT impact not confirmed

items.title: WaterISAC and CSIS: treat Iranian targeting of water as a sustained campaign, not a series of one-offs
items.content_text: WaterISAC's 11 June situation report on the heightened Iranian threat environment, reinforced by CSIS analysis, frames activity against U.S. water and critical infrastructure as a sustained campaign rather than episodic opportunistic strikes. For water and wastewater utilities the practical implication is planning posture: assume repeated probing of internet-exposed controllers and remote access, budget for continuous exposure management rather than one-time fixes, and align tabletop exercises to a persistent adversary who returns. The confirmed thread underneath the hacktivist noise remains the internet-facing PLC activity that agencies keep re-warning about.

items.title: CISA flags Frangoteam FUXA SCADA/HMI — called out for water, wastewater and energy use
items.content_text: In its 30 June batch CISA published an advisory for Frangoteam FUXA, an open-source SCADA/HMI platform, explicitly noting use in water and wastewater systems and energy. Lightweight, web-based HMI software is increasingly common at small utilities precisely because it is free and easy to stand up — which also means it can end up exposed with little hardening. Review the advisory for affected versions, apply the available fixes, and make sure any FUXA instance is off the public internet and behind authenticated access.

items.title: Delta Electronics DVP12SE PLC advisory — check pump and dosing skids that use it
items.content_text: CISA's 30 June advisories also covered the Delta Electronics DVP12SE programmable logic controller, a compact PLC found in pump, dosing and packaging skids that show up across small water and wastewater installations. Confirm whether any DVP12SE units sit in your process, apply the vendor's fixes, and ensure the controllers are not reachable from business or public networks.

items.title: FortiBleed: ~430,000 Fortinet firewalls swept for 110M+ credentials, now ransomware-linked — the way in to plant networks
items.content_text: CISA is urging Fortinet operators to act after researchers assessed the FortiBleed campaign swept roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls and SSL-VPN gateways for more than 110 million credentials, now tied to active INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware. For water utilities the risk is the remote-access path: these appliances often front vendor and operator access into plant networks, and because credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, there is no patch to apply. An attacker with valid credentials looks like a legitimate login, so treat any exposed device as compromised and rotate now.

items.title: CISA announces ANCHOR-CI for public-private infrastructure security collaboration
items.content_text: On 2 July CISA announced ANCHOR-CI, a new system to structure public-private collaboration on critical-infrastructure security. For water and wastewater operators — many of them small and under-resourced — the value of such programs is practical: faster, clearer channels to receive threat information and report incidents. Track how ANCHOR-CI intersects with WaterISAC and existing EPA/CISA reporting so your utility knows where to send and receive time-critical information.
